# What Is The Future Of Digital Twins?

![What Is The Future Of Digital Twins?](https://img2.storyblok.com/filters:format(webp)/f/122804/1600x601/b01fa0eb93/what-is-the-future-of-digital-twins.webp)

## 

An electronic duplicate of an actual thing, procedure, or framework is called a [digital twin](https://www.toobler.com/). The essence of digital twins is a digital copy of the physical object, complete with information about the object.

It is an individual one-to-one relationship to the thing, and the capacity to monitor the object is all included in that [digital twin model](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-model) or instantiation. By connecting the virtual and physical realms, it is possible to analyze data, monitor systems, and create new opportunities.

In this way, [digital twin technology](https://www.toobler.com/digital-twin-solutions) serves as a link between the real and virtual realms. It achieves this by utilizing sensors to gather data about actual objects in real-time. NASA was among the first organizations to successfully exploit the notion of digital twins, simulating space missions on Earth.

## Why Is Digital Twin Important?

**Enhanced Operational Efficiency:** Digital twins significantly increase operational efficiency by offering real-time insights into physical assets and operations.

**Predictive Maintenance:** Digital twins can help anticipate possible faults by modeling system performance. This allows for proactive maintenance to save downtime and boost efficiency.

**Cost Savings:** By streamlining resource management and cutting waste, digital twins' predictive powers result in significant cost savings.

**Optimized Product Development:** Companies can reduce expenses and development cycles by testing new concepts in digital twins before they are physically created.

**Better Customer Experience:** In businesses where customers interact with products, digital twins guarantee that they are dependable and often updated, which raises customer happiness.

**Overall Innovation:** Digital twins are essential in manufacturing, healthcare, and urban planning sectors because they support operational excellence and innovation.

## Digital Twin Technology Impact In Business

When an organization uses a digital twin, its main goal must be to use the data gathered to make decisions tailored to meet specific company objectives rather than just collecting data or information.

Since modeling and analysis are beneficial results of using digital twins, these virtual copies give much more than merely insight into the operation of distantly situated equipment. Therefore, it is essential to use the data gathered from digital twins to change the way the entire company runs.

However, business leaders need to consider collaborating with [**leading digital twin companies**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-companies).  This is to completely recognize the potential and impact of digital twins on engineering.

Additionally, they could also hire knowledgeable data analysts and technically-focused employees who have a track record of deciphering data structure. This is to support digital twin trends and intelligently drive organizational shifts.

## Future Trends of Digital Twins

As we look to the future of digital twins, we are at the forefront of the next phase in technology. Exciting things are going to happen that will change industries and go beyond conventional limits. The future scope of digital twin technology is discussed below.

**Please Read:** 10 Amazing Examples of Digital Twin Technologies for Industries. [**Here**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-examples)

### **1. Integration of Digital Twins With Extended Realty (XR)**

Users will have realistic and engaging experiences because of the combination of digital twins and extended reality (XR) technologies such as virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR).

XR platforms will offer user-friendly means of seeing and interacting with digital twins. Hence, they improve comprehension, teamwork, and decision-making. By adding digital twin data to tangible assets, engineers, operators, and maintenance personnel will be able to receive real-time advice and assistance.

XR inclusion will revolutionize learning, problem-solving, and working remotely, closing the gap between the real and virtual realms.

**Suggested Read:** How is AI Enhancing Digital Twin Capabilities? [**Here**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-and-ai)

### **2. DTaaS and Cloud-based Solutions**

Cloud-based technologies will continue to be utilized to give digital twins as a service (DTaaS). This change would increase the accessibility and affordability of digital twin technology for businesses of all kinds, democratizing its use. With flexible and adaptable solutions offered by DTaaS models, significant infrastructure expenditures will be superseded.

Cloud-based platforms will free enterprises from the headache of maintaining intricate IT infrastructure and allow them to fully utilize the promise of digital twins by providing safe data storage, processing power, and convenient access from any location.

### **3. Growth into New Markets and Domains**

![](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/2048x640/c59a0f3bd3/client-conversion.jpg)

Digital twin impacts in business have the potential to enter novel markets and sectors in addition to their current ones. They have found uses in a wide range of industries. This includes  [**digital twins in healthcare**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-healthcare), transportation, retail, energy, and aerospace, despite their early adoption predominantly occurring in these sectors.

Digital twins will transform a wide range of businesses. This ranges from controlling power systems and improving [**customer experiences through digital twins**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-customer-experience) to streamlining supply chains and monitoring patient health. Their adaptability and agility make them perfect for taking on difficult problems and promoting innovation in various industries.

**Please Read**: Digital Twins and Supply Chain Management. [**Here**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-supply-chain-management)

### **4. Sustainability and Environment-friendliness**

There will be a growing emphasis on sustainability and tackling environmental issues with digital twin technology. Businesses will use digital twins to improve resource efficiency, lower carbon emissions, and maximize energy consumption.

[**Digital twins in sustainable construction**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-sustainable-construction) are helpful in reducing environmental effects. Further,  digital twins will make it easier to design eco-friendly products and perform predictive maintenance.

[**Digital twins in smart cities**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-sustainable-construction) help develop sustainable buildings that promote better collaboration and development. They will become essential instruments in accomplishing sustainability objectives and building a more environmentally friendly future. They do this by modeling and assessing the environmental footprint of structures and procedures.

### **5. Merging Edge Computing for Real-time Analytics**

In the future, edge computing features will be incorporated into digital twins. This allows for real-time analytics and network-edge decision-making. Digital twins can react quickly to shifting circumstances and offer instant insights by analyzing information closer to the source.

Edge computing will make effective use of network resources, improve information privacy, and lower latency. This development will be beneficial in situations requiring quick decisions, such as industrial automation, smart cities, and driverless cars.

### **6. 5G Connectivity for Efficient Data Transfer**

![](https://a.storyblok.com/f/122804/2048x427/76fd840fab/prompting-customer-to-try-toobler.jpg)

The widespread deployment of 5G networks will revolutionize the potential of digital twin technology. With its fast speed, minimal latency, and comprehensive coverage, 5G will make it easier to move data between digital and physical twins.

This connection will make real-time synchronizations, remote control, and procedure and asset tracking possible. 5G's enhanced adaptability and connection would benefit sectors like autonomous vehicles, innovative production, and healthcare by opening up new avenues for productivity and development.

### **7. Data Confidentiality and Ethical Concerns in Digital Twin**

Confidentiality and ethical issues will become increasingly important as digital twin technology spreads. Issues around data ownership, confidentiality, and permission will need to be addressed by businesses and legislators.

Specific rules and regulations will be necessary to guarantee ethical and accountable utilization of digital twin technology. Encryption, access control, and data privacy will be essential for securing private data and preserving individual privacy.

Achieving a sustained adoption of digital twin technology and building public trust will require finding the ideal balance between data use and safeguarding privacy. Thus, you must connect with the [**right digital twin company**](https://www.toobler.com/blog/how-toobler-helps-companies-become-digital-twin-ready).

### 8. AI-Driven Digital Twins for Predictive Maintenance

Digital twins will increasingly use AI and ML to facilitate automated decision-making and predictive maintenance. Digital twin models will employ artificial intelligence (AI) to forecast system faults and suggest repair before problems occur. This reduces downtime and improves resource management.

This merger will result in smarter and more efficient operations in sectors including manufacturing, transportation, and healthcare. Organizations can lower expenses and increase operational efficiency by utilizing real-time data and sophisticated algorithms.

**Please Read:** [Digital Twin Security](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-security): Steps to Take & Best Practices

### 9. Customized Digital Twin Models for Medical Applications

Digital twins will be developed in the healthcare industry to provide customized models for each patient. These patient-specific models will mimic each person's biological systems in real-time, enabling individualized care and accurate treatment regimens.

[Digital twins in clinical trials](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twins-in-clinical-trials) will help anticipate possible health hazards and improve treatment results by combining medical data from multiple sources, including wearables, medical records, and genetic information.

Precision medicine will undergo a revolution due to this change, providing proactive and individualized healthcare solutions.

### 10. Ecosystems of Digital Twins for Smart Cities

As smart cities develop, digital twins will build interconnected ecosystems. This will allow different city systems, such as infrastructure, waste management, energy grids, and traffic, to be synced in real-time.

Because of these ecosystems, [digital twin smart cities](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-smart-cities) will be able to monitor, control, and improve urban activities more effectively.

For instance, to enhance city planning, lessen traffic, cut carbon footprints, and enhance the quality of life for urban dwellers, traffic patterns, energy usage, and public service performance will all be examined jointly. The incorporation of [digital twins in smart cities](https://www.toobler.com/blog/digital-twin-in-smart-city-benefits) will make new levels of efficiency and sustainability possible.

## FAQs

### 1. What is the future of digital twin technology?

With developments in AI, IoT, and data analytics propelling its expansion, digital twin technology has a bright future. Digital twins will become increasingly sophisticated and extensively integrated across industries in the coming years, facilitating automation, predictive maintenance, and real-time information.

Digital twins will boost efficiency and sustainability by supporting complicated simulations of everything from entire smart cities to renewable energy grids as processing power and data storage increase. This transition will make new opportunities for individualized solutions, quicker innovation, and robust, data-driven decision-making possible.

### 2. What is the forecast for digital twins?

The digital twin industry is expected to reach $259.32 billion by 2032 at a cumulative annual growth rate (CAGR) of 39.8%, indicating tremendous development. Increased demand for operational efficiency in industries including manufacturing, healthcare, and energy, as well as developments in IoT and AI, are important drivers.

Digital twin usage is expected to accelerate over the coming decade as businesses adopt digital transformation and realize their importance for real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and optimum performance.

### 3. How will digital twins evolve in the future predictions and emerging trends?

As AI, machine learning, and IoT integration progress, digital twins will become increasingly intelligent and self-sufficient.

Future digital twins will manage intricate simulations, ranging from supply chains to smart cities, enabling large-scale predictive maintenance and real-time monitoring.

One emerging trend is the use of digital twins in sustainability initiatives, such as maximizing energy efficiency and reducing environmental effects. Digital twins will also offer deeper insights with improved data analytics, revolutionizing business performance and decision-making.

### 4. Why will digital twins be the backbone of industry in the future?

Digital twins are well-suited to serve as the foundation of future industries. They facilitate real-time data insights, predictive maintenance, and efficiency improvements.

Digital twins enable sectors to track performance, foresee problems, and test enhancements without interfering with real-world operations by generating a virtual version of physical assets or procedures.

This technology is essential for industries adopting automation, IoT, and AI since it promotes productivity, lowers interruptions, and improves decision-making. As digital transformation advances, digital twins will promote creativity and a competitive edge in various industries.

The Future of AI and Digital Twins (AI Enabled Digital Twin): Key trends for 2024 and beyond.
=============================================================================================

The combination of digital twin technology and artificial intelligence (AI Enabled Digital Twins) is revolutionising businesses, spurring creativity, and providing fresh approaches to challenging problems. Looking ahead to 2024 and beyond, a few major themes will shape the way AI and digital twins are used in the future, giving businesses the ability to improve decision-making, streamline processes, and obtain previously unheard-of insights. As leaders in this revolution, we at Entopy assist businesses in utilising AI to unify and interpret vast amounts of disparate data from a variety of dynamic real-world environments.

1.   **Wider Adoption Across Industries**

The wider use of AI enabled digital twins in a variety of industries is one of the biggest trends for the future. Despite having historically been used in the manufacturing and urban planning sectors, the energy, logistics, and healthcare industries are starting to see the benefits of these technologies. Digital twins, for example, can be used by healthcare providers to model patient health, forecast the course of diseases, and customise therapies. AI-powered Digital Twins in logistics can simulate various scenarios and anticipate any disruptions to optimise supply chain management. Customisable, sector-specific solutions will become more and more in demand as more sectors discover the possibilities of these technologies.

1.   **Integration with IoT for Real-Time Data**

The way organisations collect and analyse data is going to change dramatically as a result of the Internet of Things (IoT) devices being integrated with AI and Digital Twins. IoT sensors have the ability to gather data from physical assets in real-time, which they can then feed into digital twins for continuous monitoring and analysis. Predictive maintenance, real-time performance tracking, and quick reaction to operational changes or emergencies are all made possible by this integration. IoT-enabled Digital Twins in the context of smart cities can enhance urban planning, optimise traffic flow, and lower energy usage to create more livable and sustainable urban settings.

1.   **Enhanced Predictive and Prescriptive Analytics**

Digital Twins will progress from predictive analytics—which forecasts what is likely to happen—to prescriptive analytics, which recommends certain actions to accomplish desired outcomes, as AI algorithms grow more advanced. Organisations will be able to anticipate future events and respond optimally in real time thanks to this evolution. Digital twins, for instance, can forecast equipment breakdowns and provide maintenance plans to avert expensive downtime in the energy sector. They are able to predict patterns in consumer behaviour in the retail industry and suggest changes to inventory in order to optimise sales.

1.   **Improved Collaboration and Decision-Making**

As they offer a virtual environment where stakeholders can interact with real-time data and simulate various scenarios, digital twins are quickly emerging as key hubs for cooperation. This capacity provides a thorough understanding of operations and possible outcomes, which improves decision-making. Future developments will see a growing integration of advanced visualisation techniques like virtual reality (VR) and augmented reality (AR) into digital twins. This will facilitate cross-location collaboration, help teams comprehend complex data, and speed up decision-making.

1.   **Focus on Sustainability and Efficiency**

Globally, companies are starting to place a high premium on sustainability, and digital twins are a potent instrument for achieving environmental objectives. Digital twins can help businesses reduce their environmental impact by increasing energy efficiency, decreasing waste, and optimising resource consumption. Digital twins, for instance, can replicate building designs to increase energy efficiency and decrease material waste in the construction industry. They can improve fertilisation and irrigation techniques in agriculture, resulting in more environmentally friendly farming methods.

1.   **Greater Emphasis on Data Privacy and Security**

Ensuring data privacy and security will be crucial as AI and Digital Twins manage more complex and sensitive data. Strong data governance frameworks must be put in place by businesses in order to safeguard data and fully utilise AI-driven insights. We could expect developments in safe data handling procedures in 2024 and beyond, such as the ability to train AI models on synthetic data without sacrificing privacy.

AI and digital twins have a bright future ahead of them since they are going to completely change how businesses function, make choices, and provide value. Companies may use AI-powered Digital Twins to spur innovation, improve operational effectiveness, and achieve sustainable growth by staying ahead of these trends. Entopy is dedicated to assisting businesses in navigating this shifting environment by giving them the resources and knowledge they require to prosper in a world that is changing quickly.

Highlights
----------

*   •Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. 
*   •The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in many industries. 
*   •The benefits of creating a Digital Twin solution are too vast and still not fully explored. 
*   •Digital Twins will combine with more technologies, such as augmented reality (AR) and AI for better connections, insights, and analytics. 

Abstract
--------

Applications of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been growing at an exponential rate, and it is transforming the way businesses operate. In the past few years, [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") leveraged vital [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and it is predicted that the technology will expand to more applications, use cases, and industries. The purpose of this paper is to do a literature review and explore how Digital Twins streamline intelligent automation in different industries. This paper defines the concept, highlights the evolution and development of Digital Twins, reviews its key enabling technologies, examines its trends and challenges, and explores its applications in different industries.

1. Introduction
---------------

[Digital Twin](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twin from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is attracting attention from practitioners and scholars alike. Today, the technology is used across many industries to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. In 2019, a Gartner survey revealed that Digital Twins were entering mainstream use by organizations. It predicted that 75 percent of [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) organizations also use [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or plan to use it by 2020 [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b1), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b2). Gartner also estimates that by 2027, over 40 percent of large companies worldwide will be using Digital Twin in their projects to increase revenue [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b3).

Moreover, Global Market Insight estimated that the Digital Twin market size estimated in 2022 at $8 billion is expected to grow at around 25 percent [Compound Annual Growth Rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about Compound Annual Growth Rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CAGR) from 2023 to 2032 [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b4). Finally, according to another recent report by global technology research, the Digital Twin market is set to grow by nearly $32 billion from 2021 to 2026 [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b5). In addition, according to a 2022 report, nearly 60 percent of executives across a broad spectrum of industry plan to incorporate Digital Twins within their operations by 2028 [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6).

Digital Twin is a cutting-edge technology that has revolutionized the industry by mirroring almost every facet of a product, process, or service. It has the potential to replicate everything in the physical world in the digital space and provide engineers with feedback from the virtual world [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7). As a result, the technology enables companies to quickly detect and solve physical problems, design and build better products, and realize value and benefits faster than previously possible. Furthermore, the Digital Twin technology enables businesses to improve business processes and performance [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b8).

The research started by developing key concepts based on a narrative literature review in the Scopus database and Google Scholar and searched for papers that included Digital Twin or Digital Twins in the title. Articles were selected from Journal articles, research articles, conference publications, research reports, or scientific encyclopedias. We reviewed the documents to find answers to the following questions: What is a Digital Twin, what technologies are used, what are the current applications of Digital Twins in different industries, and what are the challenges and opportunities facing this growing technology? We selected research papers that defined the concept, reviewed its related technologies, and highlighted its applications and trends in different industries. Section[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec2) provides the main perspectives and definitions of Digital Twins in literature. Section[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec3) reviews four enabling technologies of the Digital Twins. Section[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec4) studies Digital Twin applications and use cases of different industries. Section[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec5) highlights the challenges and opportunities of this technology. Finally, Section[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#sec6) provides a summary and conclusions. Section 7 provides references.

2. Background and definition
----------------------------

In 2003 Professor Grieves of the University of Michigan introduced the concept of [Digital Twins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin "Learn more about Digital Twins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in a total [product lifecycle management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/product-lifecycle-management "Learn more about product lifecycle management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") course. It is also known as a digital mirror and digital mapping. Since then, its definition has continued to evolve as several scholars have provided varied definitions of this technology [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b9), [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b11), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b14). Encyclopedia of Production Engineering states that “_The Digital Twin is a representation of an active unique “product” which can be a real device, object, machine, service, intangible asset, or a system consisting of a product and its related services”_[[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12)_._ In general, the Digital Twin is defined as virtual representations of [physical objects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical objects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") across the lifecycle that can be understood, learned, and reasoned with real-time data or a simulation model that acquires data from the field and triggers the operation of physical devices [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b15), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b16).

Furthermore, Digital Twin was defined as the convergence between physical and virtual products [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b18). Fu et al. [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b7) thought of the Digital Twin as a real-time digital representation of a physical object. They are remotely connected to real objects and provide rich representations of these objects. They go beyond static product designs, like CAD models, but comprise dynamic behavior [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b20). A virtual replica of a real-world asset is obtained through constant data transmission allowing the digital version of the object to exist simultaneously with the physical one [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b17). Digital Twin uses [big data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/big-data "Learn more about big data from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology to mine hidden and effective data and to improve the intelligence and applicability of [Digital Twin technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/digital-twin-technology "Learn more about Digital Twin technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), especially for quick identification and evaluation of design flaws [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b21).

Finally, Kritzinger et al. [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13) defined Digital Twin, within the field of manufacturing, based on the data integration level, which can be achieved between the physical product and its virtual representation. He identified three levels of integration, The Digital Model, Digital Shadow and the Digital Twin ([Fig.1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig1)) [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b22).

A historic early application of Digital Twin technology is when NASA engineers used a simulator, a twin of the command module, and a separate twin of the module’s [electrical system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrical-system "Learn more about electrical system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to remedy and save Apollo 13 in 1970. NASA engineers completed the process in under two hours and saved the lives of the three astronauts on board. This was an extraordinary early application of this technology, and the technology has only matured since then [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b23). Today, NASA uses Digital Twins to develop next-generation vehicles and aircraft.

The concept of Digital Twins is not new. However, Digital Twins have moved from idea to reality much faster in recent years. It is predicted that Digital Twins will be combined with more technologies such as speech capabilities, [augmented reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/augmented-reality "Learn more about augmented reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [IoT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/internet-of-things "Learn more about IoT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [artificial intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about artificial intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI). As a result, Gartner included Digital Twins on its list of top 10 technology trends for 2017 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Gartner also predicted that half of the large industrial firms to use Digital Twins in crucial [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") by 2021 [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b24). Finally, MarketsandMarkets research predicted rapid growth for the Digital Twin technology within the next few years, thanks to rising interest in the manufacturing industry to reduce cost and improve [supply chain operations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/supply-chain-operation "Learn more about supply chain operations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, the market for Digital Twin technology was valued at $6.9 billion in 2022. However, it is expected to reach $73.5 billion by 2027- a [CAGR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/compound-annual-growth-rate "Learn more about CAGR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of more than 60 percent [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b25).

3. Digital twin technologies
----------------------------

The three main aspects of Digital Twins are data acquisition, [data modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/data-modeling "Learn more about data modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and data application [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). Digital Twin uses four technologies to collect and store real-time data, obtain information to provide valuable insights, and create a digital representation of a [physical object](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/physical-object "Learn more about physical object from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These technologies include the Internet of Things (IoT), [Artificial Intelligence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/artificial-intelligence "Learn more about Artificial Intelligence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AI), [Extended Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/extended-reality "Learn more about Extended Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (XR), and Cloud ([Fig.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig2)). In addition, Digital Twin uses a particular technology, depending on the application type, to a greater or lesser extent.

*   1._Internet of Things (IoT):_ IoT refers to a giant network of connected “things”. The connection is between things–things, people–things, or people–people [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Digital Twins use IoT as its primary technology in every application. By 2027, more than 90 percent of all IoT platforms will have Digital Twinning capability [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b6). IoT uses sensors to collect data from real-world objects. The data transmitted by IoT is used to create a digital duplication of a physical object. The digital version then can then be analyzed, manipulated, and optimized. IoT constantly updates data and helps Digital Twin applications create a real-time virtual representation of a physical object. Therefore, every Digital Twin application uses IoT as a primary technology. 
*   2._[Cloud Computing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/cloud-computing "Learn more about Cloud Computing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_: Cloud computing refers to delivering hosted services over the Internet. The technology efficiently stores and accesses data over the Internet [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b27). Cloud computing provides Digital Twins with data computing technology and cloud data storage technology. Cloud computing allows Digital Twin, with large volumes of data, to store data in the virtual Cloud and easily access the required information from any location. Cloud computing enables Digital Twins to effectively reduce the [computation time](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computation-time "Learn more about computation time from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of complex systems and overcome the difficulties of storing large amounts of data [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b28). 
*   3.[Artificial Intelligence (AI)](https://softengi.com/expertise/artificial-intelligence/): As a discipline of computer science, AI seeks to mimic the basis of intelligence to create a new intelligent machine capable of responding like human-to-human intelligence. Areas of AI study include Robotics, image recognition, and language recognition. [Neural Networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/neural-network "Learn more about Neural Networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Machine Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/machine-learning "Learn more about Machine Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [Deep Learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-learning "Learn more about Deep Learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [expert systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/expert-system "Learn more about expert systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b29), AI can assist Digital Twins by providing an advanced analytical tool capable of automatically analyzing obtained data and providing valuable insights, making predictions about outcomes, and giving suggestions as to how to avoid potential problems [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b26). 
*   4.[Extended Reality (XR)](https://softengi.com/expertise/expanded-reality/) is an umbrella term used to describe immersive technologies like Virtual Reality (VR), [Augmented Reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/augmented-reality "Learn more about Augmented Reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AR), and Mixed Reality (MR). These technologies can merge the physical and virtual worlds and extend the reality we experience [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b30). XR creates digital representations of objects where digital and real-world objects co-exist and interact in real-time. Digital Twins utilize XR capabilities to digitally model physical objects, allowing users to interact with digital content.

4. Digital twins use cases and applications
-------------------------------------------

Today, engineering and manufacturing predominantly use Digital Twins to provide accurate virtual representations of objects and simulations of operational processes. Digital Twins applications in operations and supply chain management, especially the role of Digital Twins in terms of operations traceability, transport maintenance, remote assistance, asset visualization, and design [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are reviewed in related publications [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b13), [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b31), [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b32), [[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b33), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b35), [[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38). The technology is poised to deliver upon its many promises in other industries, including automotive, aerospace, construction, agriculture, mining, utilities, retail, healthcare, military, [natural resources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/natural-resources "Learn more about natural resources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and public safety sectors ([Fig.3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#fig3)) [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b12), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b19), [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b34), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b38), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b41), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b42), [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b44). The technology has captured the imagination of scholars, managers, and practitioners worldwide, and numerous [business applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/business-application "Learn more about business applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of this technology are emerging in the literature [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b10), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b47), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b48), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b49), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S277266222300005X#b50).

Highlights
----------

*   •The artificial intelligence underpinning the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT). 
*   •Vertical hierarchical computing for digital twins (DT) 
*   •A hot issue is merging DT and IIoT concepts. 
*   •Machine learning models for multivariate time series forecasting. 

Abstract
--------

The rapid emergence of the smart industry hides numerous challenges that need to be addressed promptly. In the transition between two industrial eras (Industry 4.0 and Industry 5.0), hands-on applications of digital twins in intelligent manufacturing are pivotal in enhancing efficiency, optimizing operations, and ensuring sustainability. The paper presents the digital twin (DT) concept in a vertical Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) framework powered by machine learning (ML) models for time series forecasting. According to DT needs and hierarchical data processing, as well as edge, fog, and cloud computing, the paper presents state-of-the-art ML models and algorithms. Real-time and low-latency requirements of smart edge devices and monitoring systems force the selection of DT models powered by ML models for time series processing and forecasting. Stronger computer resources characterize the IIoT fog level. At this level, DT models should be supported by techniques and methods for parameter selection, correlation analysis, and heatmap visualization that facilitates time series processing. Special attention is devoted to developing a novel multivariate-time-series prediction method. This method should enable parameter prediction which cannot be directly measured. The method was validated based on several real-time series.

1. Introduction
---------------

In the mature age of the 4th Industrial Revolution, the introduction of the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) required comprehensive changes in the industrial environment regarding networking and logical connection between computers, machines, and experts using advanced control and management strategies, fast data processing, and timely decision-making. Today, in the transitional between the 4th and 5th industrial revolutions, with the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques and IIoT technologies, digital twins (DT) have recently experienced a growth revolution [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0005). Digital twins are virtual replicas of physical systems that can mimic their actions and behaviors and aid in decision-making [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0010). Thanks to the advancements in information and communication technology, complex system optimization problems become solvable with the introduction of hands-on digital twin technology. As a result of the huge data science achievements, including cloud computing, and internet devices, DT is now widely applicable to process monitoring and data processing in both virtual and physical contexts [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0015).

Adapting traditional control and management concepts to the predominantly vertical IIoT concept [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0020) is a scientific, technological, and engineering challenge. The core of DT lies in the bidirectional link of assets (physical space) and models (virtual space). DT has accelerated the fusion of digital technology and traditional industry [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0025). As IoT has entered industrial facilities, DT has emerged as a workable and efficient approach to management, corrective control, fine parameter tuning, and early fault detection. The vertical IIoT framework predominantly comprises edge, fog, and cloud computing levels [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0020). Within the IIoT context, the DT concept has emerged as a pivotal innovation, offering unprecedented opportunities for real-time monitoring, fault prediction, parameter optimization, and power efficiency. The main advantage of the IIoT is the hierarchical processing of data and information, which also creates conditions for the parallel and simultaneous application of many DT models at the edge, fog, and cloud levels. Regarding DT networked systems, the layered architecture contains three interacting components, i.e., the physical layer, the DT layers, and the DT networks layer [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0030).

Digital twins are trying to improve existing management and monitoring systems with the support of the latest smart technologies, artificial intelligence, and real-time exchange of large amounts of data to improve monitoring, process control, and fault prediction. Smart manufacturing and maintenance are a field of research primarily driven by DT concepts that incorporate optimization methods [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0035) and theories such as: Markov decision process [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0040), Bellman principle [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0045), fuzzy decision-making, etc. DT applications for maintenance have significantly increased in the Industry 4.0 era [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0050). Furthermore, industries with manufacturing and greater maintenance costs are more interested in investing in DT technology.

The need for hierarchical architectures of DT-enabled intelligent manufacturing for practically applicable DT concepts has recently been recognized [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0055). The necessity for multi-model fusion in DT technology has also been acknowledged [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0060).

There are open challenges in IIoT, big data management, and analysis in cloud environments [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0065). However, there is a lack of functional mutual connectivity between management and decision-making concepts related to industrial IoT and digital twins. To fill this conceptual gap, this paper presents two innovations. The first pertains to the DT and IIoT integrated concepts for large industrial and power systems. The second contribution involves the selection and practical application of artificial intelligence models and algorithms that are of interest for edge and fog levels, which are lower hierarchical computing levels closer to manufacturing processes, systems, and devices.

As stated in the paper, the paper deals with strategic control, monitoring, management, and decision-making concepts in smart power plants using integrated DT models in the vertical IIoT framework. A comprehensive hierarchical DT concept with hands-on applicable state-of-the-art data science and machine learning techniques, models, and algorithms are presented.

Here, in the beginning, it is necessary to clarify the terminological dilemma, digital twin or digital twins? The authors used the term “digital twin” for a single ML model or a smaller group of ML models, while “digital twins” were used for a bunch of ML models at fog and edge levels.

The effectiveness of digital twins heavily depends on their ability to process and forecast multivariate time series data from various sensors monitoring the electricity production process and equipment. ML models, such as Long Short-Term Memory networks [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0070), Transformer-based architectures [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0075), and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), can analyze historical sensor data and predict failures before they occur.

Traditional DTs rely on simplistic predictive models for dynamic processes or equipment. Multivariate time series prediction models address a more complex approach thanks to the following:
*   •Handling High-Dimensional Data 
*   •Capturing Complex Dependencies and Interactions 
*   •Enhancing Predictive Maintenance and Decision-Making 
*   •Making DTs more adaptive and resilient 
*   •Improving DT simulations by more complex system parameters 

Multivariate forecasting techniques can predict electricity demand based on historical load patterns, weather conditions, and grid conditions. ML forecasting models enable real-time process parameter tuning, optimizing operational and maintenance efficiency. The data-driven approach enables synchronization between the dynamic model and the actual physical system, facilitating accurate predictions and efficient energy management [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0075). The digital twin platform supports the identification of potential issues and enhances overall plant performance [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0080).

2. The vertical fusion of IIoT and digital twin
-----------------------------------------------

The integration of DT into the IIoT framework is beneficial and necessary for the future of industrial automation. By bridging the gap between the physical and digital worlds, digital twins enable industries to achieve unprecedented efficiency and reliability. Here are some benefits of integrating DT with IIoT:
*   1.Performance optimization 
*   2.Downtime reduction 
*   3.Condition and probability maintenance 
*   4.Real-time monitoring [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0085) 
*   5.Anomaly and fault detection 
*   6.Aging and fault prediction 
*   7.Different real-time and parallel scenario analysis 
*   8.Cybersecurity and cyber vulnerability identification [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0090) 
*   9.Energy efficiency and cost reduction [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0095) 
*   10.Deep learning implementation 
*   11.Blockchain technology implementation [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0100) 

The IIoT is revolutionizing power generation connecting power units (generators, transformers, etc.) and processes through automation, data analytics, and distributed intelligent services [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0105). DT engineering has become a crucial methodology in future IoT and smart environments [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0110). Digital twins play a vital role in this transformation as a virtual replica of physical assets, industrial processes, and systems [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0115).

_A. The vertical IIoT concept_

IIoT devices and systems represent industrial facilities (industrial plants, large power systems, mines, factories, etc.) that generate large amounts of data [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0120). The fundamental characteristic of IIoT is the complete integration of operational and information technologies. Every smart power plant is essentially an integration of cyber-physical systems, IoT, internet services, computing at all levels (edge, fog, and cloud), wireless technology, automation, etc. The main three IIoT levels edge, fog, and cloud have their requirements, e.g., latency, privacy, or data transfer rate [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0125). The important decisions for the presented DT concept include positioning the hierarchical computing nodes [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0130). The vertical multi-level IIoT concept ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)) is defined according to the data-information-decision-making direction [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0135).

The vertical IoT concept in the industry, with clearly defined levels of edge, fog, and cloud computing, enables sophisticated data processing and optimization of industrial processes through hierarchical processing architecture. Each level plays a specific role in data collection, analysis, and utilization, allowing for more efficient decision-making and improved performance within the industrial ecosystem.

Edge computing is a paradigm for data processing and computing closest to the data sources (smart sensors, monitoring systems, and RTUs) minimizing latency and reducing the need for constant cloud connectivity. In IIoT, edge computing enables real-time data analysis and decision-making, which is critical for time-sensitive applications such as predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and process soptimization. With all the advantages of edge computing, two crucial facts are often overlooked: vulnerability to cyber-attacks and the impossibility of applying complex AI-supported decision-making algorithms.

Fog computing extends the capabilities of edge computing by providing additional processing power and storage closer to the edge but not on the device itself. It aggregates data from multiple edge devices, performs intermediate processing, and forwards relevant information to the cloud.

The cloud level is responsible for the complex data/information processing obtained from the fog (SCADA) level. At the highest IIoT hierarchical (cloud) level, complex decision-making algorithms should be based on complex ML models and ensembles. Software applications in the cloud are responsible for running AI models and decision-making algorithms.

The vertical IIoT concept is fully aligned with the in-depth security strategy. In-depth security, also known as defense in depth, is a strategy that employs multiple levels of security measures to protect an organization’s assets. If one level is breached, additional levels will remain in place to stop the threat. The highlighted grey areas ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)) between the levels contain numerous security tools such as firewalls, antivirus software, intrusion detection systems, etc.

AR and DT development strategies are the foundation for intuitively integrating human operators into the next generation of human cyber-physical systems [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0135) in the upcoming Industry 5.0 era. Today, ‘digital twins’ are a major research topic focusing on ‘smart cities’ [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0140) and ‘smart manufacturing’ [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0145), with high expectations for practical implementation in the Industry 5.0 era.

Digital twins are digital representations of physical systems or processes that serve as their digital “mirror images” created based on mathematical models for simulation, integration, testing, monitoring, and real-time maintenance [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0030). Vice versa, a Digital Twin is a dynamic, real-time, and holistic digital replica of a physical system (e.g., a power grid, a generator, or a wind farm) that is continuously updated with real-time data from sensors, IoT devices, and other sources.

It goes beyond simulation and analysis to include real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and decision-making. It is often part of a larger ecosystem that integrates AI, machine learning, and big data analytics. It can be implemented in cloud.

Using sensors and IoT technologies, data from the physical twin (production level devices) is transferred to the virtual twin, allowing engineers and managers to predict potential failures, optimize operating conditions, and improve production efficiency. The application of digital twins in the power system enables the simulation and testing of electricity generation and transmission scenarios, the integration of renewable energy sources, and the management of electricity consumption. The digital twin can continuously adapt and improve through ML models and algorithms. The state-of-the-art approach of the digital twin concepts offers the possibility of fitting into different modern optimization and management strategies [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#b0150).

_B. Digital twin concept_

In recent years, as an essential technology in the cyber-physical system, DT ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0010)) has been applied to various technological functions, phases, and cycles, including real-time simulation, remote monitoring, diagnostics, prediction, and control. Systems based on DT models and Augmented Reality (AR) are used to develop future systems based on human–machine interaction principles.

_C. The fusion concept_

Digital twins have emerged as a transformative technology in the energy sector. We are currently at an intersection between the two approaches regarding the electricity and industrial sectors. The first approach focuses on DT software tools for process and equipment control and monitoring. The second approach recognizes the need for a unified integration of DT and IIoT frameworks. This paper presents the vertical hierarchical framework of merged DT and IIoT concepts ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)), achieving a comprehensive control and monitoring approach.

The authors have identified the following shortcomings, which are presented in most research articles dealing with digital twins in an industrial environment:
*   1.Partial monitoring and modeling of systems or processes. 
*   2.Unclear relationships between the implemented models. 
*   3.Lack of unified management and decision-making strategy. 
*   4.Vulnerability to cyber-attacks and lack of in-depth security strategy. 

The proposed strategy largely overcomes the shortcomings mentioned. The main advantages of this approach are:
*   1.Hierarchical data processing and management in both DT spaces. 
*   2.Clearly defined functionalities and the merge zone between physical and virtual space (grey zone in [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)). 
*   3.Vertical in-depth security strategy with the ability to deploy independent cyber protection zones between the edge, fog, and cloud levels ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0005)). 

It is necessary to define a unique overlapping space for the physical and virtual parts of DT. Cloud computing should be functionally related to the output data and information obtained from the highest virtual twin level.

As the power industry embraces IIoT, deploying machine learning (ML) models at edge, fog, and cloud levels becomes crucial for achieving efficient and effective operations. The first two questions that need to be answered are which features and parameters should be selected and tuned to process optimization. The second step is determining feature correlation (correlation matrix) and tuning hyperparameters (accuracy, F1 score, etc.).

In the context of digital twins, the integration of ML models at edge and fog levels enhances the capabilities of these virtual replicas by providing real-time and aggregated insights:
*   1.Edge-level DT integration: 
*   •Real-time simulation: edge devices update digital twins with real-time data, enabling immediate simulation and feedback loops. 
*   •Low-level decision-making: edge ML models feed real-time data into digital twins, providing warnings and alerts enabling quick reactions to faults and anomalies. 
*   2.Fog-level DT integration: 
*   •Comprehensive analysis: fog computing aggregates data from multiple edge devices, providing a broader context for the digital twins to analyze and simulate. 
*   •Condition-based maintenance: fog-level ML models enhance in-depth analytics, helping to optimize operations and maintenance schedules. 

In the fusion concept ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0142061525002078#f0015)), cloud services and computing are responsible for complex decision-making. The presented fusion concept at the highest decision-making level relies on existing software cloud solutions and is not required to create unique DT applications. A fuzzy decision-making method should be used at the highest computational cloud level of the vertical DT concept.

3. Novel DT methodology and ML models
-------------------------------------

Nowadays, computing resources provide unprecedented opportunities for the hands-on digital twins' concept at the fog level. Machine learning models at the fog level are complex but fast enough for classification, regression, and image processing applied in quasi-real time for failure tracking, monitoring and prediction, aging prediction, and maintenance decision-making. Capital power units (generators and transformers) are managed, controlled, and monitored through many parameters (voltage, current, temperature, vibrations, flow, magnetic flux, etc.). Proper parameter selection, correlation functions (heat maps), measurement ranges and limits, output response speed to the input control signal, etc. are just some of the engineering tasks at the fog level. enriched_points.expanded_content: The convergence of AI and IoT with digital twins enhances real-time data analysis, predictive maintenance, and operational efficiency across industries, driving smarter decision-making.
Digital twins open new avenues for research in areas like personalized healthcare, environmental management, and smart city planning, fostering innovation and interdisciplinary collaborations.
Digital twins facilitate partnerships across sectors, enabling shared insights and resources that enhance sustainability practices, and optimize urban development and industrial processes.
